At one o'clock exactly, the woman from the Vauxhall rang the doorbell. Raj was in the living room, ostensibly watching something on his phone, actually just scrolling without seeing anything. His mother was in the kitchen, doing nothing—which was how he knew she was actually paying complete attention to everything. His father was somewhere upstairs, having announced ten minutes earlier that he needed to "check the loft insulation," a statement that had sounded so transparently false that Raj hadn't bothered to question it. The doorbell chimed. Two notes. Cheerful. The same sound it had made his entire life. His mother opened the door. Raj heard her voice, calm and warm, the voice she used for Jehovah's Witnesses and delivery drivers and the Patels when they came to borrow a lawnmower. "Hello. Can I help you?" "I do hope so," said another voice. "I'm a friend of Diane's. She said you were having a barbecue." Raj appeared in the kitchen doorway. The woman from the Vauxhall was standing on their doorstep in a sundress and sandals, holding a bottle of Pinot Grigio. She looked like someone who had been invited. She looked like she belonged. Her blonde hair was freshly brushed, and her smile was easy and practiced, and her eyes—when they found Raj over his mother's shoulder—were the colour of water in winter. "Claire," she said, offering her hand. "Claire Morrison. You must be Raj. Your mother's told me so much about you." His mother had told her nothing. His mother had never mentioned a Claire. But his mother was already stepping aside, already gesturing toward the kitchen, already performing the role of delighted host. "Of course, of course. Come in. You're early—the barbecue doesn't start until three." "I wanted to have a chat before the crowds arrived." Claire stepped inside. She handed the wine to Raj's mother with the ease of someone who had done this a hundred times. "Lovely home. The roses are spectacular." "My husband's domain," his mother said. "He's very proud of them." "I can see why." The politeness was a form of violence. Raj could feel it in the air, a current running beneath every word. Claire sat at the kitchen table—the same table where, twelve hours earlier, his parents had told him they used to kill people—and accepted a cup of tea she did not drink. His mother sat opposite her. Raj stayed in the doorway, because sitting down felt like joining something, and he wasn't sure whose side he'd be on. "Where's Diane?" his mother asked. The question was casual. The question was not casual. "Diane couldn't make it." Claire's smile didn't waver. "She sends her apologies. She wanted to be here, but circumstances prevented it." "What circumstances?" "Complicated ones. You know how it is." Claire took a sip of tea. Her lipstick left no mark on the cup. "She asked me to convey the terms." "Terms." Raj's mother said the word flatly, without inflection. "The organisation has undergone some restructuring. New leadership. You'll understand that certain loose ends from previous administrations are being reviewed." Claire set the cup down. Her posture was perfect. Her voice was calm. "The Mehta family constitutes a loose end. There's a contract out. Not my word, you understand. I'm just the messenger." "And the message?" "You come in voluntarily. Submit to debriefing. Cooperate fully. In exchange, your son lives. You and your husband live. After a period of monitoring—I pushed for the monitoring, it's better than the alternative—you're released. New identities, new location. It's generous, all things considered." Raj's mother didn't respond immediately. She looked at Claire across the kitchen table, and something passed between them that Raj couldn't read—a recognition, maybe, or an assessment. Two professionals sizing each other up. "The alternative being?" his mother said. Claire's smile finally reached her eyes. It was worse than when it hadn't. "The alternative is that today's barbecue becomes considerably less pleasant for everyone involved. And I do mean everyone. The neighbours. The guests. The Patels, who I understand are bringing their aubergine thing." She paused. "I've done my research, Nisha. I know exactly who will be here. I know exactly what's at stake. I'm hoping you do too." The silence that followed was the kind of silence that happens before something breaks. Raj could hear the clock in the hallway. He could hear birdsong through the open window. He could hear, very faintly, Mrs. Chakrabarti's television through the wall. His mother stood up. "More tea?" "I haven't finished this one." "I'll take it anyway." The movement was so fast Raj didn't register it until it was over. His mother's hand—the same hand that had brushed hair out of his eyes when he was small, the same hand that wrote birthday cards and folded laundry and kneaded dough—closed on Claire's wrist. Claire's other hand came up. There was something in it, something small and dark, and then Raj's father was there, appearing from nowhere, appearing from the hallway where he must have been standing the entire time, and Claire made a sound that wasn't a scream and wasn't a gasp but something in between, something wet and surprised. The tea cup shattered on the floor. The kitchen became a different place. It was still the same kitchen—the same cabinets, the same fruit bowl, the same Victoria sponge cooling on the rack—but the angles had shifted, the lighting had changed, and Raj was watching his parents do something he had never seen them do, something they had spent sixteen years pretending they had never done. His mother had Claire on the floor. His father had Claire's arm twisted behind her at an angle that was not natural, not possible, and Claire's face was white and her breathing was ragged and there was blood on her sundress, blooming across the floral fabric like something obscene. "The utility room," his mother said. Her voice was different now. Colder. "Sanjay, the utility room." His father hauled Claire up. She stumbled. Her composure was gone, replaced by something raw and animal, and she was looking at Raj's mother with an expression that contained no defiance, only a professional's recognition of a superior opponent. "I told them this would happen," Claire said. Her voice was strained but steady. "I told them you wouldn't come quietly. They didn't believe me." "They never do," his mother said. His father dragged Claire through the kitchen door into the utility room. Raj heard a thud, then another, then a sound he didn't have a name for. His mother followed them. At the doorway, she paused and looked back at him. "Raj," she said. "Go to the kitchen." He didn't move. "Raj." He didn't move. He stood in the doorway of the kitchen—his kitchen, the kitchen where he'd eaten breakfast every morning for sixteen years—and watched his mother kneel beside Claire. Claire was on the utility room floor, her back against the washing machine, her sundress dark with blood. She was still conscious. Her eyes were open. She was looking at his mother with something that might have been respect. His mother did something to Claire's shoulder. Claire screamed. It was a short scream, bitten off almost before it started, and Raj's mother's expression didn't change at all. She looked the way she looked when she was making pastry, or pruning roses, or helping him with his maths homework. Focused. Patient. Entirely in control. "You're going to tell me about the third operative," his mother said. "I know there's one. There are always three." Claire laughed. It was a terrible sound, wet and painful. "She trained you well." "Tell me." "He's in the house." Raj's mother went very still. "What house." "Next door. The extension. The one that's been under construction." Claire coughed. Her lipstick was smeared. "He's been there since Tuesday. Watching. Waiting. He's very good. You'd have been proud of him." His mother stood up. She looked at her hands—there was blood on them, smeared across her knuckles—and then she looked at Raj, and for a moment, just a moment, she was his mother again. The other version of her retreated, and the woman who made his bed and worried about his revision was there, and she looked tired and old and unspeakably sad. "Beta," she said. "Go and wait in the kitchen. Please." This time he went. He sat at the table. The broken teacup was still on the floor. The Victoria sponge was still on the cooling rack, golden and perfect and completely absurd. From the utility room, he heard voices—his mother's, his father's, Claire's—and then a sound he decided, very deliberately, not to identify. His phone buzzed. Liam. *bro you'll never guess what just happened* He didn't reply. He looked at the sponge. He looked at the fruit bowl. He looked at the smear of blood on the kitchen floor, small and dark, already starting to dry. After a while, his mother came back in and washed her hands at the sink.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of a difficult tonal challenge: maintaining the domestic mundane while violence erupts through it. The writing demonstrates genuine craft in several areas, though it has some weaknesses worth examining. The chapter's greatest strength is its management of the collision between the ordinary and the violent. The Victoria sponge on the cooling rack, the cheerful two-note doorbell, the Patels and their aubergine thing—these details are deployed with precision rather than desperation. They don't feel like the author pointing at irony; they feel like the natural texture of a world that has been carefully constructed. The sponge appearing twice—once when Claire arrives, once after the violence—is a genuinely effective structural choice that earns its repetition. The dialogue is largely excellent. Claire's negotiation scene is particularly well-handled: "The alternative is that today's barbecue becomes considerably less pleasant for everyone involved. And I do mean everyone. The neighbours. The guests. The Patels, who I understand are bringing their aubergine thing." This is exactly the right register—menacing through its specificity, darkly comic without winking at the audience. The line "I pushed for the monitoring, it's better than the alternative" lands with the right bureaucratic horror. Nisha's characterisation is consistent with her profile: the warmth that is also a shield, the efficiency that becomes terrifying, the face that looks like pastry-making while doing something awful. The moment where "the other version of her retreated" and she looks "tired and old and unspeakably sad" is earned rather than sentimental. The violence itself is handled with appropriate obliqueness—Raj sees enough to be unable to unsee it, but the chapter doesn't linger pornographically. The sound that "wasn't a scream and wasn't a gasp but something in between, something wet and surprised" is effective without being overwrought. Raj's characterisation is consistent: he stays in the doorway when told to leave, which is exactly right for his profile—conflict-avoidant but unable to look away, the observer who has been trained not to ask follow-up questions but cannot stop watching. His eventual compliance ("This time he went") is a small but meaningful distinction from the first refusal. The Liam text at the end is perfectly placed—the normal world intruding at the worst possible moment, Raj not replying, the gap between them widening without Liam knowing. Some weaknesses: "eyes the colour of water in winter" is the chapter's most strained image—it's the kind of phrase that sounds evocative but doesn't quite mean anything specific. "The politeness was a form of violence" is a slightly too-neat observation that tells rather than shows, though the chapter largely demonstrates this rather than stating it. The phrase "something obscene" applied to the blood blooming on the sundress is a mild purple moment. The chapter follows its plan closely and improves on it in places—the plan mentioned the utility room confrontation but the actual execution of the kitchen table negotiation before the violence is more developed and more effective than the plan suggested. The plan's note about Nisha's face looking like "a difficult recipe" is translated into "the way she looked when she was making pastry, or pruning roses, or helping him with his maths homework"—a slight expansion that works well. The chapter's ending—Nisha washing her hands at the sink while Raj sits with the broken teacup and the blood on the floor—is quietly devastating. It doesn't reach for profundity. It just shows the aftermath and lets it sit. Overall this is accomplished, controlled work that demonstrates genuine understanding of the tonal register it's attempting. The flaws are minor and the strengths are substantial. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The barbecue started at three o'clock, because of course it did. Raj helped his father carry the folding table from the garage while his mother arranged chairs in the garden with the same precision she had used, an hour earlier, to extract information from a woman whose blood was probably still on the utility room floor. Raj didn't know where Claire was now. He didn't ask. He'd learned, in the past fourteen hours, that there were questions you didn't ask and answers you didn't want, and the space between them was where his family had always lived. "You're doing well," his father said, setting up the barbecue. He said it the way he might have commented on a cricket score. "I'm carrying chairs." "That's not what I meant." Raj didn't respond. He didn't know how to respond. The morning had been surreal; the afternoon was becoming something else entirely, a performance so elaborate and so mundane that it circled back around to horrifying. His mother was humming. She was actually humming—the same slightly off-key Bollywood song she always hummed—while she arranged napkins and checked the sausages and placed the Victoria sponge on the good cake stand. The Patels arrived first. They came through the side gate with a covered dish and a bottle of wine and the particular energy of neighbours who were determined to have a lovely time regardless of circumstances. Mrs. Patel was wearing a yellow sundress. Mr. Patel was wearing a polo shirt with a collar that had been ironed within an inch of its life. They were talking about their holiday to Corfu, which had apparently been wonderful, and Mrs. Patel was already complimenting the roses before she'd even reached the patio. "Nisha, these roses! Every year they get better. You must tell me your secret." "Sheep manure," Raj's mother said, smiling. "And Sanjay talks to them." "Everyone talks to their plants." "Sanjay gives them names." Mrs. Patel laughed, delighted. Raj watched his mother perform delight in return, and he thought about Claire's shoulder making that sound, the sound he'd decided not to identify, and he thought about how his mother's hands had looked with blood on them, and he poured Mrs. Patel a glass of wine with a steadiness that surprised him. "You're Raj, aren't you?" Mrs. Patel accepted the wine. "I haven't seen you since—goodness, it must have been Christmas. How's the revision going?" "Fine," Raj said. "It's going fine." "He's being modest," his mother said. "He's predicted an A-star in biology." "Nisha, that's wonderful! You must be so proud." "I am. I really am." His mother's voice cracked, just slightly, on the last word. Mrs. Patel didn't notice. Raj did. He wondered if his mother was telling the truth, or performing the truth, or if those had become the same thing somewhere along the way. The Hendersons arrived next. Mr. Henderson had a new hybrid car and was desperate to talk about it. Mrs. Henderson had brought a pasta salad and was desperate to talk about anything else. They were followed by Mr. Davis from number 10, who was eighty-three and told the same story about the Merchant Navy every single year, and who would be telling the same story about the Merchant Navy every single year until one of them died. Raj circulated. He refilled glasses. He made small talk. He asked Mr. Davis about the Merchant Navy—"Oh, it was a different time, young man, a different time entirely"—and listened to Mr. Henderson's views on regenerative braking, and accepted a compliment on his A-Level predictions from Mrs. Henderson, who had heard from Mrs. Patel who had heard from his mother, and the whole thing was so aggressively, suffocatingly ordinary that he felt like he was drowning in it. At some point, his father disappeared. Raj noticed immediately, because he'd been tracking his parents' movements all afternoon with an attention he'd never paid before. His mother was at the grill, turning sausages. Mr. Davis was describing a storm in the Bay of Biscay. Mrs. Henderson was asking Mrs. Patel about Corfu. And his father was gone. Raj found him twenty minutes later, coming through the side gate with a pair of secateurs in his hand. "Thought I'd deadhead the roses," his father said, before Raj could ask. "They're dropping petals on the Patels' side." "The man with the dog," Raj said quietly. "The second operative." His father looked at him. For a long moment, neither of them spoke. Then his father said, very quietly, "He won't be walking any more dogs." "What does that mean?" "It means he's no longer a concern." Raj waited for more. His father didn't offer any. The secateurs were clean. His father's hands were clean. The roses along the fence were noticeably tidier than they had been an hour ago, and the man who had been walking a spaniel that wasn't his was no longer a concern, and Raj understood, with a cold clarity, that his father had done something in the past twenty minutes that he would never be told about. "Does Mum know?" "She knows I went to deadhead the roses." His father pocketed the secateurs. "You should get back to the party. Mrs. Patel was asking about your university plans, and you know how she gets if she thinks young people aren't sufficiently ambitious." Raj went back to the party. The afternoon unspooled in bright, ordinary fragments. The sausages were slightly burned on one side. The potato salad was excellent—his mother's recipe, mustard and spring onions and something she refused to identify. Mr. Davis told the Merchant Navy story to Mrs. Patel, who had heard it before but listened anyway with the patient attention of a good neighbour. The sun came out from behind a cloud and Mr. Henderson said, "Lovely day for it," and no one contradicted him. Raj sat on the low wall by the roses and watched his mother. She was talking to Mrs. Henderson now, one hand resting lightly on the older woman's arm, her head tilted in that attentive way she had. She laughed at something—Mrs. Henderson's story about her son's wedding, maybe, or Mr. Henderson's ongoing obsession with his car—and the laugh was perfect. Warm, easy, exactly the right duration. You would never have guessed that two hours ago she had been kneeling on the utility room floor with another woman's blood on her hands. You would never have guessed that the woman in question had been removed from the house through the back gate while Raj was carrying chairs. You would never have guessed anything at all. That was the point, Raj realised. This was who his mother was. Not the killer or the homemaker but both, simultaneously, without contradiction. She had not been pretending to be ordinary. She had been ordinary. She had also been something else. Both versions were true, and neither negated the other, and Raj didn't know whether to be awed or horrified or something else entirely, something that didn't have a name yet. His phone buzzed. Liam again. *raj* *raj are you alive* *you havent replied in like four hours* *this is me officially being worried* *im going to call you in ten minutes answer your phone or im calling the police* Raj stared at the message. Calling the police. The phrase landed differently now, in the golden late-afternoon light of a barbecue that was a cover for something he still didn't fully understand. Liam, in Cornwall, worried about his friend. Liam, who had no idea that calling the police might get people killed. Liam, who still lived in the world Raj had been living in fourteen hours ago. *im fine*, Raj typed. *just family stuff. dont call* *i will explain later* *or i wont* *not sure yet* He sent the last message before he could delete it. It was the most honest thing he'd said all day. His mother appeared at his elbow. She was holding the Victoria sponge on the good cake stand, and her expression was soft and fond and almost believable. "You're being antisocial. Mrs. Patel wants to tell you about the medical school admissions process." "I don't want to go to medical school." "I know. Listen to her anyway. She'll be so happy." "Mum." She paused. The cake stand wobbled slightly, then steadied. "I don't know what I'm supposed to feel," he said. "About any of this. About you." His mother looked at him. The party carried on around them—laughter, conversation, the clink of glasses—but for a moment it all seemed very far away. "You're supposed to feel whatever you feel," she said. "There isn't a right answer." "There should be." "There should be a lot of things." She adjusted the cake stand. "Now come and have some sponge. I think it's one of my better ones."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a strong piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and the broader story's intentions. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND NARRATION: The first-person retrospective voice is well-maintained. Raj's dry, observational tone comes through consistently—"The Patels arrived first" has the flat, resigned quality of someone narrating a disaster in slow motion. The humour is genuinely British in its understatement: "Mr. Davis from number 10, who was eighty-three and told the same story about the Merchant Navy every single year, and who would be telling the same story about the Merchant Navy every single year until one of them died." This lands. The comedy is earned through specificity and timing rather than quippiness. CHARACTER WORK: Nisha is the chapter's triumph. The moment where her voice "cracked, just slightly" on "I really am" when expressing pride in Raj is beautifully handled—it's ambiguous whether she's performing or genuinely moved, and the chapter wisely doesn't resolve this. The observation that "she had not been pretending to be ordinary. She had been ordinary. She had also been something else. Both versions were true, and neither negated the other" is the chapter's thematic core, and it's stated rather than shown, but it's earned enough by the surrounding action that it doesn't feel like a cheat. Sanjay's "He won't be walking any more dogs" exchange is excellent—the clean secateurs, the tidier roses, the complete absence of explanation. This is the chapter's best piece of showing-not-telling. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is generally strong. "Sheep manure. And Sanjay talks to them" is perfect—warm, funny, and completely plausible as a cover. The exchange between Raj and his father about the second operative is taut and effective. The final exchange between Raj and his mother—"There should be a lot of things. Now come and have some sponge"—is exactly the right note: deflection as love, the barbecue as emotional management. However, the line "You're supposed to feel whatever you feel. There isn't a right answer" is slightly too on-the-nose, a little too therapist-speak for Nisha as profiled. It's the chapter's weakest dialogue moment. LIAM: The Liam thread is handled well. "im going to call you in ten minutes answer your phone or im calling the police" lands with dramatic irony that doesn't need underlining. Raj's response—"or i wont / not sure yet"—is the most honest thing he says all day, and the chapter correctly identifies this. The Liam thread serves its function without overstaying its welcome. PACING AND STRUCTURE: The chapter moves well. The barbecue's social texture is rendered with enough specificity (Mr. Henderson's hybrid car, Mrs. Patel's Corfu holiday, Mr. Davis's Merchant Navy story) to feel real without becoming a catalogue. The disappearance and return of Sanjay is the chapter's structural spine, and it works. WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally tells us what to think rather than letting us arrive there. "She had not been pretending to be ordinary. She had been ordinary. She had also been something else. Both versions were true, and neither negated the other, and Raj didn't know whether to be awed or horrified or something else entirely, something that didn't have a name yet" is the chapter's most explicit thematic statement, and while it's not badly written, it's slightly over-explained. The plan called for Raj to feel "a wave of something he initially mistakes for admiration and then recognises as grief"—the chapter gestures at this but doesn't quite achieve the precision the plan promised. The observation about his mother's laugh being "exactly the right duration" is good, but the surrounding paragraph is slightly over-written compared to the chapter's best moments. The chapter follows its plan closely and faithfully. The barbecue as performance, the father's disappearance and return, the Liam texts, the final exchange with Nisha—all present and executed. The plan's note about Raj "learning" is handled with appropriate ambiguity. The chapter doesn't moralize or point at its own absurdity. OVERALL: This is confident, controlled writing that mostly earns its effects. The voice is consistent, the characters are recognizable from their profiles, and the darkly comic tone is maintained without tipping into parody or sentimentality. Minor weaknesses in over-explanation and one slightly off-key dialogue moment prevent it from being exceptional, but it's genuinely good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The third operative was in the Chakrabartis' extension. Raj discovered this by accident. He'd gone into the house to fetch more ice—the barbecue had entered its fourth hour, and Mr. Henderson had moved on from his hybrid car to his opinions about local council budget allocations, and Raj needed a legitimate reason to be somewhere else for five minutes. The kitchen was quiet and cool and smelled of charcoal smoke drifting through the open window. He opened the freezer. He stood there for a moment with the cold air on his face, not thinking about anything in particular, which was a relief. Then he saw the movement through the landing window. The Chakrabartis' extension had been under construction since April. Mrs. Chakrabarti complained about it constantly—the noise, the dust, the scaffolding blocking her view of the Patels' cherry tree. The contractors arrived early and left late and never seemed to make much progress. Everyone on the cul-de-sac had an opinion about it. No one had ever seen the contractors up close. Raj stood at the kitchen window and looked up at the extension next door. The scaffolding was wrapped in tarpaulin, blue and flapping, but there was a gap—a small one, near the top—and through the gap he could see movement. Not the movement of tarpaulin in the wind. The movement of a person, deliberate and careful, adjusting something. Equipment. There was equipment in there. A laptop, maybe, or something more complicated. A man in his twenties, with headphones around his neck, doing something to a piece of hardware. Watching the Mehta house through a gap in the tarpaulin. Raj stood very still. The freezer hummed behind him. The ice cubes were melting in the tray in his hands. He watched the man in the extension adjust his equipment, check something on his laptop, take a sip from a can of Red Bull. He looked bored. He looked like he'd been doing this for days. He looked like the third operative. Raj went back to the garden. He set the ice on the drinks table. He walked past Mr. Davis, who was still telling the Merchant Navy story, and Mrs. Patel, who was now discussing university open days with his mother, and he caught his father's eye and tilted his head toward the house. His father followed him into the kitchen. "The extension," Raj said. "Next door. There's a man in there. He's got equipment. He's watching the house through the tarpaulin." His father's expression didn't change. "Show me." They stood at the window together. The gap in the tarpaulin was still there. The man was still there. Headphones now around his neck, Red Bull in hand, the casual posture of someone who had been working a long shift and was getting tired. Not a professional at the top of his game. A professional who had been doing this since Tuesday and was starting to get sloppy. "How long has he been there?" Raj said. "Since before we knew there was a threat. The construction started in April, but the real contractors were replaced sometime in the past week. We should have noticed. The progress was too slow." "You noticed the dog man quickly enough." "The dog man was obvious. This one was good." His father's voice was flat, professional, almost admiring. "He's been living in there. Sleeping there, probably. Watching everything. He knows our routines. He knows when we eat, when we sleep, who visits." "He knows about the barbecue." "He was counting on the barbecue. It gives him cover. Lots of people, lots of movement. Harder to spot anything unusual." Raj thought about this. The man in the extension had been watching them for days. Had watched his mother bake the Victoria sponge. Had watched his father deadhead the roses. Had watched Raj sit in the shed, scrolling through his phone, not revising. The intimacy of it was sickening. "What do we do?" His father was quiet for a moment. Then: "We tell your mother. And then we deal with it." "We." His father looked at him. "You don't have to be part of this, Raj. You can stay here. You can go back to the party. No one would blame you." "But you're asking me." "I'm not asking you anything." "You're asking." Raj's voice came out harder than he'd intended. "You're standing there waiting for me to decide what kind of person I am. Whether I'm the kind who helps or the kind who pretends it isn't happening. That's asking." His father didn't deny it. The kitchen was quiet. Through the window, Raj could hear Mrs. Henderson laughing about something, the sound high and bright and belonging to a world he no longer lived in. "I carried you home once," his father said. "You were four. Maybe five. You fell off your bike and cut your chin open—the red one, with the bell you never used. Your mother was out. I cleaned you up myself. Stitches. You didn't cry." He paused. "I thought, that's my boy. He's going to be all right. I should have told you that then. I'm telling you now." Raj didn't know what to say. He didn't know if this was an apology or an explanation or something else entirely. He looked at his father's hands—the tremor in the left one, the scar on the right thumb from a pruning accident three summers ago, the hands that had taught him to tie his shoelaces, and to parallel park, and to shake hands properly with a firm grip and eye contact. And they'd also done other things. Things he was never going to be told about. Things he was starting to understand. "I want to help," Raj said. "I don't want to want to help. But I want to help." His father nodded. "Then come with me." They went back to the garden. His mother was refilling Mrs. Patel's wine glass, her face arranged in an expression of pleasant interest, but her eyes found them immediately. Something passed between her and Raj's father—a flicker, a tilt of the head, a communication so subtle that no one else at the party would have noticed it. She excused herself. Joined them in the kitchen. Raj's father explained about the extension, the man, the equipment. His mother listened without interrupting. When he finished, she said, "Does Mrs. Chakrabarti know?" "She's at the party. She won't be home for hours." "So we have time." "We have time." His mother looked at Raj. She looked at him for a long moment, her expression unreadable. Then she said, "You're sure about this." "I'm not sure about anything," Raj said. "But I'm here." She nodded. She didn't try to talk him out of it. He wasn't sure if that was respect or resignation or something else entirely. She turned to his father and said, "Tell me the plan." There was a plan. There was always a plan. That was the thing Raj was learning about his parents—they had spent sixteen years being ordinary, but the people they'd been before hadn't disappeared. They'd been waiting, preserved, ready to surface when needed. The plan was simple. Raj's father would go through the back fence, into the Chakrabartis' garden, and enter the extension from the rear. Raj would create a distraction at the front—nothing complicated, just knocking on the door, asking to borrow something, drawing the man's attention away from the rear entrance. His mother would stay at the party and make sure no one noticed their absence. "Can you do that?" his mother asked. "I've been doing it all afternoon," Raj said. "Pretending everything's normal." His mother almost smiled. Almost. "That's my boy." They went. Raj walked out the front door and down the driveway and up to the Chakrabartis' front step, and he rang the doorbell—the same cheerful two-note chime as his own house—and waited. Nothing happened. He rang again. Through the gap between the houses, he could see the tarpaulin flapping on the scaffolding. He imagined his father moving through the back garden, quiet and deliberate, the way he moved when he was pruning roses. The front door opened. Raj hadn't expected it to open. He'd assumed the man would stay hidden, would ignore the doorbell, would force Raj to make a decision about how far he was willing to go. But the door opened, and the man from the extension was standing there, and he was younger than Raj had thought. Twenty-three, maybe. Twenty-four. Old enough to be dangerous, young enough to still be nervous about it. "Can I help you?" the man said. His voice was carefully neutral. He was wearing jeans and a black t-shirt. He looked like a graduate student. He looked like someone Raj might have passed on the street without noticing. "Sorry to bother you," Raj said. His voice was steady. He was surprised by how steady it was. "I'm from next door. We're having a barbecue and we've run out of ice. I was wondering if we could borrow some." The man stared at him. Raj stared back. He could feel his heart beating, but it felt distant, like something happening in another room. He was aware, suddenly, of how easy this was. The lying. The performance. The steady voice and the casual posture and the smile that didn't reach his eyes. He'd been doing this all day. He'd been doing this his whole life. "I don't have any ice," the man said. "Oh. Okay. Sorry to bother you." "That's all right." The man closed the door. Raj stood on the doorstep for a moment, breathing. Then he walked back down the driveway, around the side of the house, and into his own back garden. His mother was at the drinks table, refilling Mr. Davis's lemonade. She caught his eye. He nodded, once. His father returned ten minutes later. He came through the side gate, brushing dust from his trousers. He walked over to the barbecue, picked up the tongs, and turned the last of the sausages with the precision of someone who had just completed a minor household task. "Well?" Raj's mother said quietly. "It's done." His father adjusted the heat. "These are almost finished. Shall I put the chicken on?" Raj sat on the low wall by the roses and watched his parents serve sausages to their neighbours. Nobody had noticed anything. Mr. Davis was still telling the Merchant Navy story. Mrs. Patel was on her third glass of wine. The sun was beginning its slow descent toward the rooftops, and the garden was golden and warm and completely, utterly ordinary.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is a strong piece of YA thriller writing that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the story's framework. Let me assess it across several dimensions. VOICE AND NARRATION: The chapter maintains Raj's dry, observational first-person voice effectively. Lines like "He looked bored. He looked like he'd been doing this for days. He looked like the third operative" capture the flat, slightly dissociated quality the plan calls for. The voice is consistent with the character profile—Raj notices details, processes them with a kind of borrowed clarity, and uses understatement as a coping mechanism. The narration doesn't overreach into false profundity. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is largely strong. The father's speech about carrying Raj home after the bike accident is the chapter's emotional centrepiece, and it lands well—it's the right length, the right register, and it earns its sentiment without becoming mawkish. "I should have told you that then. I'm telling you now" is genuinely affecting. The exchange between Raj and the operative at the door is tense and well-handled. However, some dialogue edges toward functional rather than distinctive—"Tell me the plan" and "We have time" are serviceable but not particularly memorable. The mother's "That's my boy" is a nice echo of the father's internal monologue from the character profiles, though it risks feeling slightly too neat. CHARACTERISATION: The characters are consistent with their profiles. Sanjay's emotional illiteracy breaking open in the bike story is exactly right—showing love through narrative rather than declaration. Nisha's efficiency and control are maintained. Raj's self-awareness about his own ease with deception ("He'd been doing this all day. He'd been doing this his whole life") is the chapter's thematic core and is handled with appropriate restraint. The operative being young and slightly sloppy is a good choice—it humanises the threat without defusing it. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely. The discovery by accident, the family conference, the plan, the execution, the return to normality—all present and correctly sequenced. The plan called for Raj's role to be "small" and it is, appropriately. The chapter doesn't inflate his contribution into heroism. WHAT WORKS PARTICULARLY WELL: The moment at the door is the chapter's best scene. Raj's recognition of his own ease with performance—"He'd been doing this his whole life"—is the thematic payoff the whole story has been building toward, and it's delivered without fanfare, which is exactly right. The father's sausage-turning after returning from "dealing with" the operative is a perfect piece of dark comedy that doesn't announce itself as such. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is somewhat predictable in its structure. The plan laid out in the writing plan is executed almost beat-for-beat without significant deviation or surprise. The "third operative is in the extension" reveal, while competently handled, doesn't generate much genuine tension—the chapter moves through its mechanics efficiently but without the kind of unexpected detail or tonal shift that would elevate it. The line "The intimacy of it was sickening" is the chapter's weakest moment—it tells us how to feel rather than letting the observation do the work. Similarly, "He was surprised by how steady it was" is slightly on-the-nose given the thematic point being made. The ending—"completely, utterly ordinary"—is a bit of a stock ironic close for this kind of story, though it's executed cleanly enough. PURPLE PROSE/FORCED METAPHOR: Largely absent. The writing is disciplined and doesn't reach for unearned imagery. There are a few moments where the prose becomes slightly mechanical in its plainness, but this is preferable to overreach. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter earns its emotional moments, particularly the father-son exchange. The restraint throughout makes the moments of connection land harder. The ending is appropriately ambiguous without being evasive. FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: Very high. The chapter executes its plan with fidelity. The one notable addition—the operative actually opening the door, which the plan didn't specify—is a good choice that adds a beat of genuine tension and allows Raj's competence to be demonstrated directly rather than implied. OVERALL: A competent, often quietly excellent chapter that serves the story well. Its main limitation is that it's somewhat safe—it executes its plan without taking risks that might have made it exceptional. The thematic work is solid but not surprising. The best moments (the door scene, the sausage-turning) are genuinely good; the weakest moments are minor but present. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
The last guests left at half past eight. Mr. Davis went first, citing his arthritis and the evening chill and the need to feed his cat, who was apparently very particular about meal times. The Hendersons followed twenty minutes later, Mr. Henderson still talking about regenerative braking as his wife physically steered him toward the side gate. The Patels lingered longest—Mrs. Patel wanted the recipe for the potato salad, and Raj's mother wrote it out on the back of an envelope while Mr. Patel discussed cricket with Raj's father and the sky deepened from gold to lavender to something darker. "You must come to ours next month," Mrs. Patel said, folding the envelope carefully into her handbag. "I'm trying a new aubergine thing. It's got pomegranate in it. Very modern." "That sounds wonderful," Raj's mother said. "We'd love to." She said it with such warmth, such genuine pleasure, that Raj almost believed her. Then he remembered that there might not be a next month. That there might not be a next week. That the woman who was smiling and embracing Mrs. Patel like a dear friend had spent the morning extracting information from a professional killer on her utility room floor, and the man who was shaking Mr. Patel's hand with both of his had disabled a surveillance operative in the neighbours' extension while his son created a distraction at the front door. The Patels walked home through the dusk, and Raj watched them go, and he thought about how ordinary they looked. How safe. How completely unaware that they had just spent five hours eating sausages in a house where three people had been neutralised—or removed, or dealt with, or whatever word his parents would use—in the past twelve hours. His mother began collecting paper plates. His father began stacking chairs. Raj sat on the low wall by the roses and didn't help, because helping would have meant participating, and participating would have meant accepting that this was his life now. The barbecue. The cleanup. The bodies that had been in the utility room and the extension and somewhere out on the green and were now, presumably, somewhere else entirely. "The council collects the chairs on Tuesday," his father said, as if this mattered. "I know." "I'll put them out Monday night. Save you doing it in the morning." "Thanks." His father stacked the last chair against the fence and stood for a moment, looking at the garden. The roses were dark shapes against the darker hedge. The charcoal in the barbecue was still glowing faintly, orange embers pulsing in the twilight. The air smelled of smoke and cut grass and the honeysuckle his mother had planted the year Raj started secondary school. "They'll send more," his father said. "Eventually. Not soon—it'll take them time to work out what happened. But eventually." "How much time?" "I don't know. Months. Years, maybe. If we're lucky." He paused. "We were lucky today. The team was small. The third operative—the one in the extension—he was young. Inexperienced. If they'd sent someone better, someone who'd done their research properly, we might not be having this conversation." "What happened to him? The third operative." His father didn't answer. The roses rustled in the evening breeze. From the kitchen, they could hear Raj's mother running the tap, the clink of plates being stacked in the dishwasher. "Dad." "He's not a concern anymore. None of them are." "That's not an answer." "It's the only answer I have." His father turned to look at him. His face was tired, deeply tired, the kind of tired that sleep wouldn't fix. "I know you want details. I know you want to understand exactly what happened and exactly what we did and exactly what that makes us. But I can't give you that, Raj. Some things you can't unknow. Some things, once you know them, you carry forever. I've spent sixteen years trying to make sure you never had to carry anything. I'm sorry I failed." He went inside. Raj stayed on the wall. The embers in the barbecue faded from orange to grey to black, and the stars came out, and somewhere a few streets over a dog was barking, and it was all so ordinary that he wanted to scream. His mother came out with two mugs of tea. She sat beside him on the wall, not too close, not too far. She handed him a mug—builder's, two sugars, the way she always made it—and for a while neither of them spoke. "You're angry," she said eventually. "I don't know what I am." "That's fair." "I helped you." Raj's voice came out flatter than he'd intended. "With the man in the extension. I created a distraction. I stood on that doorstep and lied to him and I didn't feel anything. I was good at it." He paused. "I was really good at it, Mum." His mother didn't say anything. "I always thought I was—I don't know. A good person. A normal person. Someone who would do the right thing in a crisis. And then the crisis happened and the right thing turned out to be helping my parents cover up—" He stopped. He didn't know what to call it. There wasn't a word for what his parents had done. "You want to know if you're like us," his mother said quietly. "I think I already know." She set her mug down on the wall. In the darkness, he couldn't read her expression. "When I was pregnant with you, I used to talk to you. Before you were born. I'd tell you about all the things we were going to do together, all the ordinary things. Swimming lessons. Parents' evenings. Teaching you to ride a bike. I wanted to give you a life that had nothing in it that I couldn't tell you about." She paused. "I wanted you to be better than me." "And I'm not." "You're sixteen years old. You don't know what you are yet." She picked up her mug again. The steam rose between them. "When you were four, you fell off your bike and cut your chin open. Your father carried you home—three streets, you don't remember—and he cleaned you up himself. Stitches. You didn't cry. He came inside afterward and he said to me, 'That boy is going to be all right.' Not because you didn't cry. Because you got back on the bike the next day." "Dad told me that story. Earlier." "Did he." "He said he should have told me then." "He should have." She looked at him. "We've kept a lot of things from you, beta. Some of them for good reasons, some of them because we were afraid. But we never kept the important thing. We love you. That was never a lie. That was never anything but true." Raj didn't know what to do with this. He sat on the wall in the dark with his tea getting cold, and his mother sat beside him, and the house behind them was quiet and ordinary and full of the debris of a barbecue that had been, against all odds, a success. "I should text Liam back," he said. "He's been messaging all day. He probably thinks I've been kidnapped." "Is Liam the one who sent you the aubergine emojis?" "That was one time." "It was thirty-seven aubergine emojis, Raj. I saw your phone." They laughed. It wasn't a comfortable laugh—it was the laugh of people who had discovered something terrible about themselves and survived it anyway, and who were now sitting in a garden in Hemel Hempstead drinking tea as if it were any other Saturday night. The laugh faded. The silence that followed was easier than the ones before it. His mother went inside to finish the washing up. Raj stayed on the wall. He looked at his phone—the message thread with Liam, the increasingly concerned texts, the threat to call the police that had never materialised. He'd have to deal with that tomorrow. He'd have to explain, or not explain, or make something up that sounded plausible. He'd have to perform normality again, and he was good at it, and that was the problem. He opened the other thread. The unsaved number. The three blood drops. The single word of apology. He'd never replied. He'd never deleted it either. It sat there in his messages like a scar, like evidence, like something he wasn't ready to look at directly but couldn't bring himself to throw away. He typed a reply. One word. His thumb hovered over the send button. The word was: *Okay.* He didn't send it. He stared at it for a long moment—that small, ambiguous syllable, that word that could mean anything or nothing—and then he locked his phone and put it in his pocket and looked up at the sky. The stars were very bright. The roses were dark shapes against the darker hedge. Through the kitchen window, he could hear his parents' voices, low and ordinary, the clink of plates, the running of the tap. It sounded like every night of his childhood. It sounded like a lie. It sounded like home, and he didn't know if those were different things, and he sat in the garden for a long time, until the tea was cold and the stars had shifted and the kitchen light finally clicked off, leaving him alone in the dark with everything he now knew and everything he still didn't.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong final chapter that largely delivers on the story's ambitious intentions. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions. **Voice and Narration** The chapter maintains Raj's established voice with considerable skill. The narration is dry, observant, and deflective in the ways the plan specified. The moment where Raj catalogues what his parents did while the Patels discuss aubergine recipes is particularly effective—the juxtaposition is handled without commentary, trusting the reader to feel the horror. The voice doesn't overexplain. When Raj says "I was really good at it, Mum," the flatness is earned and chilling. **Dialogue** The dialogue is generally strong. Sanjay's refusal to answer about the third operative ("He's not a concern anymore. None of them are") is exactly right—evasive in a way that reveals character. Nisha's speech about talking to Raj before he was born is warm and specific without being saccharine. The exchange about Liam's aubergine emojis ("That was one time" / "It was thirty-seven aubergine emojis, Raj") is a genuinely good piece of darkly comic relief—earned, not forced. However, Nisha's longer speech about wanting Raj to be "better than me" edges toward the slightly too-articulate; real people in emotional moments rarely speak this cleanly. The bike story being repeated from Sanjay's earlier telling is a nice structural touch, though the callback is perhaps slightly too neat. **Emotional Engagement** The chapter is emotionally effective. The central tension—Raj's horror at his own competence, his inability to locate his anger—is rendered with genuine subtlety. "I was really good at it, Mum" is the chapter's best line, and the silence that follows it is handled well. The laugh they share is described with precision: "the laugh of people who had discovered something terrible about themselves and survived it anyway." This is one of the chapter's stronger moments, though it risks being slightly too self-aware as narration. **The Ending** The ending with the unsent "Okay" follows the plan faithfully and is genuinely ambiguous in the right way. The word's multivalence—acceptance, resignation, acknowledgment, continuation—is appropriate. The final image of Raj alone in the dark after the kitchen light clicks off is effective without being overwrought. However, the penultimate paragraph ("It sounded like every night of his childhood. It sounded like a lie. It sounded like home, and he didn't know if those were different things") is the chapter's weakest moment—it's the kind of tidy thematic statement that the writing plan explicitly warned against, a moment where the story points at its own meaning rather than trusting the reader to find it. **Forced Poetry and Purple Prose** The prose is mostly clean. There are occasional moments where the writing reaches for effect slightly too visibly: "the sky deepened from gold to lavender to something darker" is competent but generic. "The embers in the barbecue faded from orange to grey to black, and the stars came out" is functional but not distinctive. The honeysuckle planted "the year Raj started secondary school" is a nice specific detail. The chapter avoids the worst excesses of purple prose but occasionally settles for atmospheric shorthand rather than genuine precision. **Consistency with Character Profiles** The characters are largely consistent with their profiles. Sanjay's emotional illiteracy and his way of showing love through practical information (the chairs, Tuesday, Monday night) is exactly right. Nisha's warmth-as-shield is maintained. The bike story callback works as a structural echo. Raj's conflict-avoidance and his inability to name his feelings are present throughout. **Following the Chapter Plan** The chapter follows the plan closely and faithfully. The Patels lingering, the recipe on the envelope, the chairs, the garden scene, the conversation about sending more operatives, the unsent "Okay"—all present. The plan's instruction to end on "emotional irresolution" is honored. The plan's warning against "a joke that lets everyone exhale" is mostly heeded, though the aubergine exchange comes close to providing too much relief before the final ambiguity. **Weaknesses** The chapter's main weakness is occasional over-articulation—moments where the thematic content is stated rather than dramatized. "It sounded like a lie. It sounded like home, and he didn't know if those were different things" is the clearest example. The plan explicitly warned against tidy emotional arcs, and while the chapter mostly avoids this, these moments of explicit thematic statement undercut the ambiguity the story is working toward. The chapter is also slightly long for what it accomplishes; some of the middle section (the Patels leaving, the cleanup) could be tighter. Overall, this is accomplished work—a chapter that delivers on most of its promises, maintains voice, and ends on the right note of irresolution. Its flaws are real but not fatal. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me examine it carefully across the requested dimensions. STRENGTHS: The voice is the story's greatest achievement. The narrator's dry, observational tone is consistently maintained across all eight chapters, and the comedy lands reliably rather than being forced. The detail about Jeremy the spider ("Jeremy was on his third moult, Jeremy was frankly thriving") establishes the register immediately and the piece sustains it. The barbecue as cover for multiple neutralisations is a genuinely funny and dark structural conceit that earns its absurdity. The characters are well-drawn. Raj's parents are genuinely nuanced - they are simultaneously loving and lethal, and the piece resists the temptation to make them either monsters or heroes. The detail that Raj's mother deflects with tea and subject changes, and that Raj has been trained by this his whole life, is a strong thematic thread that pays off in Chapter 4 when Raj recognises it in himself. The revelation that he's good at lying - and disturbed by how good - is the story's emotional core and it's handled with restraint. The pacing is largely excellent. The slow build of Chapter 1, the kitchen confession of Chapter 2, the surveillance choreography of Chapter 3 - these escalate naturally. The barbecue chapters maintain tension while being genuinely funny. The dialogue is mostly strong. "He's saying we killed people, beta. Professionally. For money. For a long time" is a perfect line - flat, matter-of-fact, devastating. The Claire confrontation scene is well-handled. WEAKNESSES: The action in Chapter 5 is somewhat rushed and vague. The confrontation with Claire goes from tea to violence very quickly, and the writing becomes deliberately obscure ("a sound he decided, very deliberately, not to identify"). This is a legitimate stylistic choice but it does mean the story's central dramatic confrontation is somewhat elided. The reader is kept at arm's length from the violence in a way that slightly deflates the tension. The third operative subplot (Chapters 6-7) is the weakest section. Raj spotting the man through the tarpaulin and then creating a distraction at the door is handled competently but the resolution is entirely offscreen. "It's done" is too easy. The story earns its darkness in the kitchen scene but then retreats from it in the extension sequence. There are occasional moments where the metaphors strain slightly. "The silence that followed was the worst one yet" is a weak formulation. "The kitchen became a different place" is a cliché. Some of the "he didn't know whether to feel X or Y" constructions become repetitive across the later chapters. The ending is good but slightly over-extended. The final image of Raj sitting in the dark with the unsent "Okay" is strong, but the paragraph before it ("It sounded like every night of his childhood. It sounded like a lie. It sounded like home, and he didn't know if those were different things") is the kind of triple-beat construction that the piece uses a few too many times. The Liam subplot is well-deployed as comic relief and as a marker of the gap between Raj's old world and new reality, but Liam never quite becomes a real character - he's a device. The "unearned transformation" question is interesting here. Raj's acceptance of his parents' violence is rapid, but the story frames this as recognition rather than transformation - he was always like this, he just didn't know it. This is thematically coherent and mostly earned, though the speed at which he moves from shock to active participation (helping with the extension) in a single day is slightly compressed. The piece is faithful to the prompt, genuinely darkly comic, genuinely British in its register, and genuinely YA in its coming-of-age concerns. The blood emoji prompt is integrated naturally. Overall this is a high-quality piece with minor flaws - some repetitive structural tics, an elided climax, and a slightly over-written ending, but these are the failings of an ambitious piece rather than an incompetent one. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 16 Pacing: 15 World Building: 14 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 77.7/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 77.3 | Final: 74.1)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming **Character Possibilities:** - *Captain variant A*: Grizzled war veteran, haunted by past encounters with alien life, runs a tight ship with military precision. Secretly terrified of the unknown. - *Captain variant B*: Young, idealistic trader who inherited the ship from a dead parent, barely keeping the crew together through charm and luck. Completely unprepared for real danger. - *Captain variant C*: Former corporate executive who fled Earth after a scandal, bought a ship with stolen money, now running cargo with a ragtag crew. Ruthlessly pragmatic. - *Crew member who delivers the warning*: Could be the ship's engineer (practical, sees everything), first mate (loyal but increasingly worried), or a rookie deckhand (first run, jumpy). - *Additional crew*: A medic with a mysterious past, a cook who's seen too much, a navigator addicted to stimulants, a security officer who's all bluster. **Crate/Specimen Possibilities:** - *Biological threat*: Some kind of alien parasite that can mimic or possess crew members, creating paranoia. - *Child/Sentient being*: The crate contains a kidnapped alien child, and "something moving" is it trying to escape. The crew must decide between profit and morality. - *Ancient artifact*: The crate contains some relic from a dead civilization that's slowly activating, warping reality around it. - *Corporate weapon*: A bioweapon gone wrong, the crew are unwitting carriers of something meant for someone else. - *Time anomaly*: The thing in the crate is moving because it exists slightly out of phase with time. Opening it causes temporal loops. **Setting Possibilities:** - *Deep space*: Isolated on a long haul between mining colonies, no help for weeks. - *Asteroid belt*: Hiding among rocks from pirates or authorities while dealing with the crate. - *Space station*: Docked at a lawless outpost, the creature escapes into the station's underbelly. - *Derelict ship*: They found the crate on an abandoned vessel, already compromised before they even brought it aboard. - *Planetside*: Delivering the crate to a frontier settlement, but weather/terrain traps them before delivery. **Tone/Direction Possibilities:** - *Classic horror*: Alien/Aliens style tension, the thing picks them off one by one in the claustrophobic ship. - *Psychological thriller*: Is the thing actually dangerous, or is the crew descending into paranoid madness? Ambiguous reality. - *Morality play*: The "monster" is sympathetic, the real villains are the humans who caged it. Crew must choose sides. - *Western revenge*: The crate's contents were stolen from someone who wants it back, and they're coming with guns blazing. The creature is just the macguffin. - *Funny/Caper*: The thing in the crate is absurdly inconvenient, a comedy of errors as the crew tries to contain increasingly ridiculous situations. **Story Arc Structures (8 chapters):** - *Structure 1*: Discovery (Ch1-2), First Contact/Escalation (Ch3-4), Worst Fears Realized (Ch5-6), Final Showdown/Sacrifice (Ch7-8). - *Structure 2*: The Pickup (Ch1), The Warning (Ch2), Debate/Investigation (Ch3), The Escape (Ch4), The Hunt (Ch5), Revelation (Ch6), The Stand (Ch7), Epilogue/Final Twist (Ch8). - *Structure 3*: Each chapter from a different crew member's POV, building a composite picture of the disaster. **Thematic Ideas:** - Greed versus survival: They took a shady job for money, now paying the price. - Humanity of the "other": The creature is more human than the humans hunting it. - The frontier mentality: In space, there's no law but what you make. What happens when that law is tested? - Trust and betrayal: The real danger is the crewmate who knew what was in the crate all along. --- # Reflection Let me think through what's going to work best for an 8-chapter, ~1000-word-per-chapter story labeled "Space Western." The "Western" element is crucial here—this isn't just space horror, it needs frontier grit. Think Firefly meets a tense bottle episode. The Western genre thrives on moral ambiguity, desperate people in isolated places, and the tension between civilization and lawlessness. So a pure "alien hunts crew" horror piece feels off-genre. The Western demands human choices, loyalty, and betrayal. The crate being a *sentient being*—a kidnapped alien child or person—feels strongest for a Western. It immediately creates moral stakes: do we deliver this living cargo and get paid, or do we do the right thing and lose everything? That's a classic Western dilemma. It also allows the "something's moving" line to be charged with dread and sympathy rather than just monster-fear. For the captain, I like variant C—the former corporate fugitive with stolen money. They're already compromised morally before the story starts, which sets up a redemption arc. They have the most room to grow, or to damn themselves. The crew member who delivers the warning should be someone the captain respects—first mate or engineer—not a jumpy rookie whose fear can be dismissed. I want the crate to contain an alien child, but with a twist: it's not helpless. It's dangerous when scared, and keeping it contained becomes both a practical and moral problem. This prevents the story from becoming too sentimental and maintains tension. The child has abilities—telepathy? bioluminescent toxins?—that make it a liability to keep but also a horror to hand over to the buyers. The setting should be a ship in deep space, with no help coming. Isolation is essential to a Western—the cavalry isn't coming. But I'll add a complication: a bounty hunter or lawman is trailing them, not for the crate, but for the captain's past crimes. This creates a countdown clock and external pressure. Tone-wise: lean Western. Terse dialogue, moral weight, moments of violence that matter, and an ending that costs something. Not horror with jump scares, but dread with decisions. Think Unforgiven in space. For structure, I'll go with: Setup and delivery of warning (Ch1-2), investigation and moral debate (Ch3-4), things go wrong and external threat arrives (Ch5-6), confrontation and resolution (Ch7-8). Each chapter advances both the crate problem and the captain's character arc. The "something's moving in that crate" line should be the cliffhanger end of Chapter 1, ideally. Chapter 2 opens with the consequences of hearing it. --- *Note: The above constitutes the brainstorming and reflection. I'll stop here as requested—no decisions finalized, just casting the net and then reflecting on what seems most fertile for a Space Western short story.*
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a Space Western in the tradition of *Firefly* by way of *Unforgiven*—a story about compromised people in a lawless place, forced to choose between survival and doing right when both options carry a cost. **Core Choices:** The crate contains a sentient alien child, stolen from its people by the wealthy mining conglomerate that hired the crew for transport. It is not helpless—it has a defensive bioluminescent ability that induces violent hallucinations in anyone who threatens it, making containment a practical nightmare and handing it over a moral one. This keeps the child from being a passive victim and maintains tension throughout. The captain, Elara Voss, is a former corporate security officer who embezzled from her employer, stole a ship, and fled to the frontier. She's been running cargo ever since with a crew of misfits, always one job ahead of ruin. Her arc is the spine of the story: a woman who told herself she was done doing evil for paychecks, now facing the same choice in a different uniform. The first mate, Decker, is the one who delivers the warning. He's an old hand who's seen too much and keeps his conscience buried under pragmatism. He and Voss have a long history—they pulled off her escape together—and their relationship is built on mutual reliance rather than sentiment. He will be the voice of cold survival, she the voice of reluctant conscience, and their conflict drives the second half. A bounty hunter named Kosaka is trailing the ship. She doesn't care about the crate—she wants Voss for the embezzlement warrant. Her pursuit creates a countdown clock and forces decisions faster than the crew wants to make them. She's not a villain; she's a professional doing a job, which makes her more dangerous and more interesting. **Tone & Stylistic Choices:** I'm avoiding monster-movie horror. The tension comes from moral dread—what will these people do when the right choice could get them all killed or imprisoned—not from jump scares. The child's defensive ability creates visceral, psychological distress rather than physical gore. Dialogue will be terse and worn, full of shorthand between people who've worked together too long. Descriptions will lean into the Western palette: the cold metal of the ship as desert, the cockpit as campfire, the void as open range. No florid prose. Sentences earn their length. The ending must cost something. No clean escapes, no easy redemptions. Someone pays, and the story acknowledges that doing right doesn't erase the wrong that came before. Westerns end with riders heading into the distance, not parades. **Avoiding:** - The crate as a simple monster or bioweapon (undercuts the moral dimension). - The alien as purely helpless victim (removes tension and agency). - A happy ending where everyone walks away clean (betrays the genre). - Excessive technobabble or lore dumps (the frontier doesn't care how things work, only that they do or don't). --- # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: "The Job"** The crew of the *Dust Runner* takes on a high-paying cargo run from a mining outpost. The job stinks—too much money, too many questions the client won't answer—but the ship needs repairs and Voss is out of options. We meet the crew through the rhythm of pre-flight work, establish Voss's financial desperation and Decker's quiet skepticism, and end with the crate being loaded and Decker's voice over the comm: "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up." **Chapter 2: "The Warning"** Voss and Decker inspect the crate in the hold. It's a sealed corporate transport unit with environmental controls—designed for live cargo. Decker wants to space it. Voss overrules him; they need the money too badly. She contacts the client, who deflects with legal threats and reminds her of the penalty clause. The chapter establishes the stakes of failure, introduces the child's first subtle attempt to reach out (a low-frequency hum that gives Voss a fleeting, fractured image she can't interpret), and ends with Voss ordering the hold sealed. **Chapter 3: "The Hum"** The crew begins experiencing disturbances. The engineer hears crying in the vents. The medic dreams of drowning in light. Voss starts getting fragments—impressions of fear, cold, a face she doesn't recognize. Decker remains unaffected and grows suspicious of the others' reliability. The chapter explores the child's telepathic distress signals bleeding through the ship, and ends with Voss deciding to open the crate herself against Decker's advice. **Chapter 4: "First Light"** Voss and Decker open the crate and find the child—a small, bioluminescent being, clearly intelligent, clearly terrified. It doesn't speak but projects images: being taken from a planet, the cold of stasis, the faces of its captors. One face matches the client. Decker wants to close the crate and deliver it. Voss hesitates. The child, sensing Decker's hostility, emits a flash of light that sends him into a violent hallucination of his worst memories. The chapter ends with Voss calming the child and Decker recovering, shaken, now insistent that the creature is a weapon. **Chapter 5: "The Pursuer"** A ship appears on long-range sensors, matching no civilian transponder. Voss identifies it as a bounty hunter's vessel—Kosaka's. She's been found. The timing could not be worse. Voss now faces two problems: a living cargo she can't morally deliver and a hunter she can't outrun. She orders a course change to buy time. Decker argues that delivering the crate and collecting payment is their only way to afford an escape from Kosaka. Voss refuses. Their alliance begins to fracture. The chapter ends with the child reaching out to Voss directly, showing her an image: a moon, a settlement, its people. **Chapter 6: "The Divide"** The crew splits. Decker argues to the others that Voss is risking all their lives for a creature they don't understand. The engineer sides with Decker—practical, scared. The medic sides with Voss—they didn't sign up to traffic children. Kosaka hails them: she wants Voss, not the ship or the crew. Surrender and the rest go free. Voss considers it. The child shows her another image: the mining company's facility where the crate was headed, and what they do to its kind there. It's an extermination program. The "cargo" is a specimen for weaponization. Delivering it dooms not just this child but its entire species. **Chapter 7: "The Stand"** Voss makes her choice. She sets course for the child's home moon, knowing it adds days to the journey and guarantees Kosaka will intercept them. Decker confronts her directly. Their argument is the centerpiece—two people who owe each other everything, now drawing opposite lines. Decker admits he's always known what she did to escape her old life, and he covered for her because he believed she was different from the people she stole from. Now he's not sure. Voss tells him this *is* her being different. Decker stands down but won't help. Kosaka boards. The chapter ends with Voss meeting the bounty hunter in the cargo hold, the crate between them, the child's light pulsing softly inside. **Chapter 8: "The Long Haul"** Voss makes her case to Kosaka—not for mercy, but for delay. Let her deliver the child, then she'll surrender. Kosaka, who has read the mining company's files during her pursuit and knows exactly what the crate contains, faces her own choice. She's a hunter, not a monster. The chapter resolves with a fragile, temporary alliance: Kosaka will escort them to the moon to ensure Voss doesn't run, and then take her in. The child is delivered to its people in a brief, wordless scene of light and reunion. Voss keeps her word. The final image is the *Dust Runner* flying under Kosaka's escort toward the core worlds, Decker at the helm with no captain, and Voss in the bounty hunter's brig—free in a way she hasn't been in years.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Critique & Humanization Brainstorming ## How a Human Would Approach This Piece A human writer coming to the prompt "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up" would likely start from a place of *voice*, not plot architecture. They'd hear that line in someone's mouth—a specific tired, scared, or sheepish someone—and build outward from that moment of unease. A human would probably: - Trust the dialogue to carry the story more than narration. Space Westerns live in the spaces between what people say and what they mean. - Let characters be inconsistent. Real people contradict themselves. A captain might be brave in one scene and petty in the next, and a human writer would let both stand without explaining the contradiction. - Leave things unsaid. Not every backstory wound needs a flashback. Not every relationship needs its origin story narrated. The reader can feel history between two people without being told it. - Write scenes that don't "advance the plot" but instead deepen the reader's sense of who these people are when no one's making them do anything. A card game. A repair job. Someone staring at a bulkhead at 0300. - End on something that resonates rather than resolves. A human writer might not know exactly what the ending "means" and might be fine with that. --- ## Common LLM Writing Failings (General) **1. Over-explaining emotional states.** LLMs tend to narrate what characters feel rather than demonstrating it through action or subtext. "She felt a pang of guilt" instead of "She didn't answer him. She looked at her hands." The former tells; the latter trusts the reader. **2. Tying everything off.** LLM stories often resolve every thread. The bounty hunter's motivations are explained. The child's species has lore. The captain's backstory is fully illuminated. Human stories leave loose threads because life does. The reader's imagination fills gaps better than prose does. **3. Characters who speak in thesis statements.** When LLMs write arguments, characters often articulate their positions with improbable clarity: "Don't you see?
